lens.included_activities: Independent US manufacturers' representatives, wholesale agents, merchandise and commodity brokers, commission merchants, group purchasing organizations acting as agents, and B2B electronic markets that repeatedly arrange goods transactions for external principals or buyers without taking title.
lens.excluded_activities: Merchant distributors that take title, captive internal sales forces, firms whose main activity is logistics or consulting rather than arranging goods sales, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat outsourced market access, account coverage, sourcing, negotiation, transaction coordination, and purchasing aggregation paid through commissions or fees tied to represented lines, customers, transactions, memberships, or purchasing volume.

a.bridge: The NAICS definition and BLS wage data identify wholesale sales representation as central, while O*NET describes the occupation's tasks. I map product and price Q&A, prospecting, account research, quote and contract preparation, records, follow-ups, reporting, forecasting, and routine matching to AI exposure, then reduce for face-to-face trust, negotiation, technical judgment, travel, relationship ownership, and complex accountability. O*NET is not an industry time study and may overstate substitution where relationships are the broker's actual asset.
a.rationale: Agents and brokers usually operate from offices and do not own or physically handle goods, leaving a larger share of labor in communication, search, matching, records, proposals, and coordination than merchant distribution. Much of that information work is AI-addressable, but closing, negotiation, line strategy, customer trust, and technical selling remain difficult to substitute.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task diary across independent rep agencies, brokers, GPOs, and electronic markets, split by product complexity and showing current automation, time allocation, and revenue responsibility for each role.
caveats: No public source reports wage-weighted AI task exposure for NAICS 425120.
The code spans technical representatives, commodity brokers, GPOs, and digital markets with very different task mixes.
Exposure is limited to current not-yet-automated tasks and is therefore below raw technical capability.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures whether employer businesses use AI in any business function, not the fraction of exposed wholesale-broker labor implemented. I use the 17-20% current and 20-23% near-term expected economy-wide adoption rates as an initial anchor, then allow five years for AI to diffuse through CRM, quoting, prospecting, product search, meeting, document, and reporting tools. This may understate adoption in digitally native brokers and overstate it in relationship-led small agencies with fragmented principal systems.
rho.rationale: Office-based, document- and communication-heavy workflows are comparatively implementable, and a large customer-support field study demonstrates meaningful assisted productivity. Full implementation is constrained by separate principal systems, data rights, commission attribution, hallucination and product-liability risk, customer consent, sales-process change, and the need to protect relationship quality.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of agencies and brokerage platforms reporting deployed workflows, active-user rates, human review, errors, conversion outcomes, hours saved, headcount avoided, and changes in principal or customer retention.
caveats: BTOS usage is an all-business binary measure, not industry implementation intensity.
The customer-support field experiment is adjacent to inside sales but not wholesale brokerage.
Implementation that improves selling rather than avoids labor should not be counted as substitution.

e.rationale: The NAICS model itself is an outsourced service to external buyers or sellers and repeat relationships are common, so many independent firms qualify. Eligibility is reduced by captive or single-principal arrangements, owner-only books of business, nonassignable rep agreements, commodity businesses with episodic transactions, platform economics unlike transferable service firms, and uncertainty in the injected EBITDA-band mapping.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 5,160 estimated band firms showing revenue recognition, commission versus facilitated-sales amounts, independent ownership, principal and customer concentration, contract assignability, recurring revenue, owner dependence, and normalized EBITDA.
caveats: The injected firm count uses a distributor margin bridge even though agents and brokers earn commissions or fees and do not take title, which may materially misclassify the EBITDA band.
Establishment classification does not establish firm-level independence or transferability.
A single-principal agency may be economically captive even if legally independent.

s5.bridge: Census research reports that 72.7% of mature employer firms in the 2022 ABS had at least one owner age 55 or older, while the manufacturers' representative association treats succession planning as a live agency governance issue. I translate age pressure into a five-year qualifying control-transfer probability after discounting continued operation, internal succession, closures, nonassignable principal contracts, founder-linked relationships, and small agencies that cannot transfer. The age statistic is all-industry and likely overstates transferable supply if this code includes many owner-personal books.
s5.rationale: Aging mature-firm ownership supports potential deal flow, but the asset in a rep or broker firm may reside in personal trust and revocable principal agreements. Control transfers therefore require stronger continuity planning than physical-asset businesses, and owner exit can instead produce contract reassignment or dissolution.
s5.change_evidence: An association and broker survey of agency owners linked to five years of closed control sales, internal successions, contract terminations, and closures, including buyer type and post-close retention of principals and customers.
caveats: Owner age is not an industry-specific sale rate.
Succession guidance demonstrates relevance but supplies no transaction denominator.
Transfers that only reassign a line or customer book without control of an operating firm do not qualify.

q.rationale: Commission or fee billing does not mechanically pass labor savings through, allowing efficient firms to retain early benefit as higher account capacity or operating margin. However, principals can renegotiate commission rates, reallocate territories, terminate lines, build direct sales, or demand more service; buyers can shift to digital matching and procurement platforms; and electronic markets face visible fee competition. The five-year retained share is therefore moderate with a wide range.
q.change_evidence: Agency-contract and transaction-level panels showing commission or fee changes, renewal and termination outcomes, service-scope changes, principal concentration, platform take rates, and competitor responses before and after productivity deployments.
caveats: No public study directly measures pass-through of AI-enabled benefit in wholesale agency agreements.
Commission protection, termination terms, territories, and line ownership vary widely by state, contract, and product.
The estimate excludes demand displacement and implementation risk.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034, while CBO projects real GDP growth averaging 1.6% annually from 2026 to 2031. I treat the sales-representative projection as a closer but labor- and productivity-affected proxy and GDP as an upper-level goods-transaction anchor, producing a wide five-year constant-quality demand range around flat. Employment can understate service quantity when productivity rises and GDP can overstate intermediary demand when trade goes direct.
d5.rationale: Underlying goods trade and manufacturers' need for market coverage support continued demand, especially where independent reps provide established buyer access at variable rather than fixed sales cost. Direct-to-buyer tools, manufacturer consolidation, procurement platforms, customer data access, trade volatility, and automated matching can reduce demand for the current service basket even as goods volumes grow.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar commission and fee cohorts for 425120 split among manufacturers' reps, commodity brokers, GPOs, and electronic markets, reconciled to underlying goods volume and channel share rather than nominal transaction value.
caveats: The BLS projection is for an occupation across industries, not industry service demand.
Real GDP is not wholesale transaction volume and does not capture intermediation share.
The code spans end markets with different cycles, regulation, and direct-channel risk.

o.rationale: Complex products, negotiated terms, principal conflicts, customer trust, territory development, credit or supply exceptions, and accountable representation preserve a human-led operator role. Yet the NAICS definition explicitly includes Internet and electronic means for matching buyers and sellers, so standardized search, matching, quoting, ordering, and reporting can become self-service or software-supplied. The operator-required share is materially lower than in wholesalers that own and fulfill physical inventory.
o.change_evidence: Product-level channel studies tracking transactions migrating from human agencies to direct manufacturer sales, customer self-service, electronic markets, and AI agents, while identifying who remains contractually accountable for recommendations, negotiation, compliance, and post-sale resolution.
caveats: No observed source isolates future quantity that must retain a lens-type accountable operator.
Technical and relationship-intensive lines are more durable than standardized catalog or commodity transactions.
A digital brokerage can remain an operator under this NAICS if it bears service accountability; entirely self-served software volume does not.

narrative.executive_view: Wholesale agents and brokers are information- and relationship-intensive outsourced sales and sourcing businesses with little physical handling, creating substantial AI exposure and relatively implementable workflows. The same digital characteristics also create direct-channel, electronic-market, fee-compression, and software-displacement risk, while transferability depends on retaining principals and customer relationships after an owner exits.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can research accounts, match buyers and sellers, maintain CRM, draft outreach and proposals, answer routine product questions, prepare quotes and contracts, summarize meetings, forecast pipelines, reconcile commissions, and automate principal reporting. Humans remain important for trust, technical judgment, negotiation, territory development, conflict management, and accountable representation.
narrative.value_capture: Commission and fee structures initially decouple savings from customer billing, but principals and buyers can observe expanded capacity and use renewals, territory changes, direct channels, or platform alternatives to claim value. Durable retention depends on proprietary relationships, difficult market access, technical depth, and multi-line synergies rather than routine transaction administration.
narrative.firm_availability: The outsourced model is definitionally aligned with the lens, but captive economics, owner-personal books, revocable or nonassignable agreements, and digital-market variation reduce eligibility. Mature-owner demographics support succession supply, yet an owner's departure can dissolve value unless principals, customers, staff, systems, and agreements survive the transfer.
narrative.demand_durability: Underlying goods trade should remain substantial and many manufacturers prefer variable-cost external coverage from representatives already established with buyers. Growth is limited by direct selling, customer self-service, manufacturer and buyer consolidation, procurement platforms, and automated matching, so current-service demand is near flat in the base case with a wide downside and upside range.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest issue is measurement: public data combine human agencies, commodity brokers, GPOs, and electronic markets, while the injected firm count applies a distributor margin bridge to a commission/fee industry. Task exposure is high but may create augmentation rather than avoided labor, and both value retention and transfer probability hinge on private contracts that public datasets do not observe.
narrative.principal_driver: A large office-based sales, matching, research, coordination, and reporting workload that can be AI-assisted without owning inventory.
narrative.principal_weakness: Digital tools can erode the intermediary's necessity and fee while owner-linked principal and customer relationships can fail to transfer.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 425120 Wholesale Trade Agents and Brokers
sources.supports: Defines agents and brokers as acting for buyers or sellers, including through Internet or electronic means, not taking title, and receiving commissions or fees; examples include independent and manufacturers' representatives and agent GPOs.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Wholesale and Manufacturing Sales Representatives
sources.supports: Projects 1% occupational employment growth from 2024 to 2034, reports 1.614 million jobs in 2024, identifies 142,100 annual openings, and shows commission-based compensation and industry-specific wages for wholesale agents and brokers.

sources.title: O*NET: Sales Representatives, Wholesale and Manufacturing, Except Technical and Scientific Products
sources.supports: Documents core tasks including customer product and price questions, product recommendations, quotes and terms, post-sale support, contracts, records, competitor monitoring, prospecting, and demonstrations.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Census BTOS data for December 2025-May 2026 report overall business AI use of 17-20% and expected use in the next six months of 20-23%, with significant variation by size and sector.

sources.title: Generative AI at Work
sources.supports: A field study of 5,179 customer-support agents finds an average 14% productivity gain from an AI assistant, with a 34% gain for novice and lower-skilled workers.

sources.title: About the Annual Wholesale Trade Survey
sources.supports: Explains that agent, broker, and electronic-market companies report sales, commissions, and operating expenses, distinguishing their economics from title-taking merchant wholesalers.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: Wholesale Trade sector
sources.supports: States that wholesale follow-up orders often reflect long-standing seller-buyer ties and that agents, brokers, electronic markets, commission merchants, GPOs, and manufacturers' representatives generally operate from offices and do not own or handle goods.

sources.title: Financing, Ownership, and Performance
sources.supports: Census research reports that 72.7% of mature employer firms in the 2022 ABS had at least one owner age 55 or older.

sources.title: Selecting the Right Manufacturers' Representative
sources.supports: Describes manufacturers outsourcing sales to agencies with established buyer relationships and trusted-problem-solver reputations, and identifies succession plans, line cards, CRM, reporting, forecasting, customer references, and agreement terms as diligence topics.

sources.title: The Budget and Economic Outlook: economic outlook for 2026 to 2031
sources.supports: Projects continued US economic expansion from 2026 through 2031, with real GDP growth averaging 1.6% annually.
